prompt:
(working on LN-based nonlinear photonics):
Possible ways to mitigate the material damage of LN after plasma etching?
article:
Assumptions (to make this actionable): you’re fabricating *thin‑film LN / LNOI* waveguides/resonators for χ(2) nonlinear photonics (telecom/NIR), and the “damage” you care about is whatever increases optical loss, reduces Q, degrades high‑field reliability (DC drift/charging), or worsens photorefraction after *plasma etching (ICP‑RIE/RIE)*.

## 1) What “plasma‑etch damage” usually means in LN photonics (so you can target the right fix)

### A. Sidewall roughness + micromasking + redeposition (dominant practical limiter)
For etched LN photonics, the most consistently documented loss mechanism is **scattering from rough sidewalls**, where roughness is often created/amplified by **micromasking** and **redeposition of non‑volatile etched material** during physical (Ar) sputter etching or mixed plasmas. This is explicitly called out as a key limiter: optical losses are limited by scattering from sidewall roughness introduced by micro‑masking effects, in part due to redeposition on etched walls [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11501321/). Redeposition can even be a **different phase/composition** than crystalline LN, visible in FIB/SEM contrast [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11501321/).

A closely related issue is the “redep layer” described as **amorphous material stuck to nanostructures**, which many groups treat as something to either (i) prevent during etch, or (ii) remove after etch—but removal can itself roughen sidewalls if done aggressively [[14]](https://www.db-thueringen.de/servlets/MCRFileNodeServlet/dbt_derivate_00070614/ome-15-2-299.pdf), [[21]](https://arxiv.org/html/2407.09208v1).

### B. Chemistry‑specific residues (especially fluorine → LiF / LiFx‑type byproducts)
Fluorine chemistries are often implicated in **hard, nonvolatile byproducts**:
* Fluorine plasmas can form/redeposit **LiF** on LN sidewalls, making etching harder and increasing roughness/scattering [[2]](https://hal.science/hal-03183505v1/file/oe-26-4-4421.pdf), [[13]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11194688/).
* Fluorine exposure can also yield nonvolatile fluorinated residues (e.g., LiFx salts / fluoropolymers) identified by XPS in at least one recent study (preprint) [[3]](https://assets-eu.researchsquare.com/files/rs-5775859/v1_covered_bb4f8f8c-5cc6-4189-b443-456f929df438.pdf).

### C. Ion‑bombardment material modification (near‑surface disorder, charging, altered stoichiometry)
Even when residues are controlled, energetic ions can modify the near surface (disorder, point defects), and you may see this indirectly as broadened XPS peaks for Nb/Li after etch (preprint evidence) [[3]](https://assets-eu.researchsquare.com/files/rs-5775859/v1_covered_bb4f8f8c-5cc6-4189-b443-456f929df438.pdf). Practically, this can couple into:
* **photorefractive (PR) behavior** (often interface/state‑driven in thin‑film LN),
* **EO drift / charging** and long‑time‑constant instabilities (strongly interface/process dependent) [[3]](https://assets-eu.researchsquare.com/files/rs-5775859/v1_covered_bb4f8f8c-5cc6-4189-b443-456f929df438.pdf),
* and additional absorption/scattering beyond pure geometric roughness.

### D. “Material loss” in thin‑film LN can be anneal‑recoverable
Independent of sidewalls, ion‑sliced thin‑film LN can show material‑loss components that are **improved by post‑fabrication annealing**, with reported material‑limited Q improved to ~1.6×10^8 (≈0.2 dB/m) [[6]](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2022APLP....7h1301S/abstract), [[7]](https://pubs.aip.org/aip/app/article/7/8/081301/2835188/Reduced-material-loss-in-thin-film-lithium-niobate). Even if your dominant loss is roughness, this sets an important target: once scattering is low, **material/defect loss** matters and is often anneal‑sensitive.

---

## 2) Highest‑leverage strategy: prevent the damage/redeposit during etch (so post‑etch “repairs” don’t have to be aggressive)

Even though you asked “after plasma etching,” in LN the best “post‑etch damage mitigation” is often **process design that avoids creating thick redeposition/damaged layers**, because some cleaning/smoothing steps can themselves increase roughness or introduce new interface states [[14]](https://www.db-thueringen.de/servlets/MCRFileNodeServlet/dbt_derivate_00070614/ome-15-2-299.pdf), [[21]](https://arxiv.org/html/2407.09208v1).

### A. Tune Ar ICP‑RIE sputter etches into a “redeposition‑free regime”
For **Ar sputter etching**, a detailed study proposes reaching a **redeposition‑free regime** during etch and gives three practical levers:  
1) **Balance DC bias and chamber pressure** (their most direct knob set) [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11501321/).  
2) Use **feature proximity** (nearby structures can deflect ions and increase lateral components that help clear redeposits) [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11501321/).  
3) Use **trapezoidal mask shaping** to bias redeposition dynamics toward removal instead of accumulation [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11501321/).

They also quantify that raising DC bias can substantially reduce redeposition area (nearly 4× smaller at 800 V vs 100 V in their tests) [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11501321/). The tradeoff is that higher ion energy can increase trenching and other ion‑damage modes, so you’re optimizing “redep removal” vs “bombardment damage” (and mask erosion).

**Practical add‑on:** use **endpoint control** to reduce unnecessary over‑etch exposure. Interferometric endpointing was used to keep etch depth consistent (200–250 nm) while comparing conditions [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11501321/).

### B. Use low‑pressure windows that suppress redeposition but keep plasma stable
In an x‑cut LNOI rib waveguide process, redeposition became unmanageable at higher pressures, while too low pressure destabilized plasma; **~2 mTorr** gave the most consistent results in that system [[15]](https://ksp.etri.re.kr/ksp/article/file/70162.pdf). This kind of “sweet spot” is tool‑dependent, but the mechanism is general: higher pressure increases collisions and can worsen redeposition/micromasking; too low pressure can destabilize discharge and/or change ion angular distributions.

### C. Substrate temperature control can reduce residues and/or improve selectivity
Two different “temperature levers” show up in recent LN etch flows:
* Cooling the substrate (example: **5 °C**) was used to enhance mask selectivity in Ar‑based ICP‑RIE [[15]](https://ksp.etri.re.kr/ksp/article/file/70162.pdf).
* In a **CHF3 ICP‑RIE** wafer‑scale process, lowering the electrode temperature was reported to eliminate visible residuals on etched structures (interpreted as reaction‑kinetics/adsorption control allowing pump‑out of byproducts) [[14]](https://www.db-thueringen.de/servlets/MCRFileNodeServlet/dbt_derivate_00070614/ome-15-2-299.pdf), [[21]](https://arxiv.org/html/2407.09208v1).

### D. Chemistry choice: Ar (physical) vs fluorine (chemical) is a trade space
* Ar sputter etching is repeatedly treated as the “optical‑quality default” but redeposition and sidewall angle are chronic issues [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11501321/), [[13]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11194688/).
* Fluorine chemistries can create LiF‑related problems [[2]](https://hal.science/hal-03183505v1/file/oe-26-4-4421.pdf), [[13]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11194688/), but can also deliver excellent results when combined with strong process controls and cleaning/anneal; one wafer‑scale CHF3 process reports straight‑waveguide loss ~**2 dB/m** with structures “free of residuals and redeposition” after optimization + cleaning + anneal [[14]](https://www.db-thueringen.de/servlets/MCRFileNodeServlet/dbt_derivate_00070614/ome-15-2-299.pdf), [[21]](https://arxiv.org/html/2407.09208v1).

In other words, “post‑etch damage mitigation” may start with deciding whether you want to **pay** for residue risk (fluorine) or **pay** for redeposition/angle risk (Ar), then build the corresponding post‑etch recovery flow.

---

## 3) Post‑etch recovery: remove redeposition/contamination **without** roughening sidewalls

### A. Wet cleaning targeted to redeposition removal (but chosen to avoid LN roughening)
A consistent theme is that **redep removal is crucial** for low‑loss LN waveguides in real tool environments [[13]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11194688/). One concrete redeposition‑removal recipe (used after Ar ICP‑RIE on x‑cut LNOI) is:
* **NH4OH : H2O2 = 1:1**, 85 °C, **1 hour**, reported to remove byproducts and reduce sidewall roughness [[15]](https://ksp.etri.re.kr/ksp/article/file/70162.pdf).

**Cautions / tradeoffs**
* Some cleaning agents can worsen roughness (preprint evidence notes standard cleans like HF or SC‑1 can roughen TFLN surfaces) [[9]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.01913v3). So, even when a clean removes residues, you should validate via AFM/SEM and optical Q statistics rather than assume improvement.
* HF‑containing chemistries can attack buried SiO2 in LNOI stacks (critical if you use wet etches as part of smoothing) [[29]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0925346721006340).

### B. Megasonic + acid mixtures to remove passivation/residual layers (fluorine‑etch flows)
A wafer‑scale CHF3 ICP‑RIE process combined multiple wet steps after etch, including:
* Cr removal by a ceric ammonium nitrate/perchloric acid mixture
* then an additional acid clean in megasonics (phosphoric + nitric + acetic acids, 50 °C, 1 hour) described as removing passivation and yielding smooth sidewalls [[14]](https://www.db-thueringen.de/servlets/MCRFileNodeServlet/dbt_derivate_00070614/ome-15-2-299.pdf), [[21]](https://arxiv.org/html/2407.09208v1).

This is a “high‑throughput/production‑style” answer to redeposition damage: accept a chemistry that can leave passivation, then build a robust residue strip.

### C. Oxygen plasma / UV‑ozone / solvent cycling for organics and light redeposits
A 2025 source explicitly lists **oxygen plasma, UV/ozone, and solvent cycling** as methods to remove hydrocarbon residues or redeposited materials originating from dry etching [[30]](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/lpor.202502309). These are especially useful when:
* your dominant post‑etch contamination is **organic** (resist residues, fluoropolymer films),
* you want to avoid aggressive wet chemistries that risk etching LN/SiO2 or increasing roughness.

(You still need to confirm that your oxygen plasma settings don’t create new surface charging/interface states that worsen PR or DC drift in your specific device stack.)

---

## 4) Post‑etch recovery: heal the *material* (crystallinity/defects) with annealing

### A. Oxygen annealing is widely used to reduce loss by improving LN quality
In a wafer‑scale etched LNOI platform, an **O2 anneal at 520 °C for 2 hours** was reported to reduce waveguide loss by “improving the crystallinity of the LN” [[14]](https://www.db-thueringen.de/servlets/MCRFileNodeServlet/dbt_derivate_00070614/ome-15-2-299.pdf), [[21]](https://arxiv.org/html/2407.09208v1). Separately, thin‑film LN material‑limited loss can be improved dramatically by post‑fabrication annealing, reaching ~**Q ≈ 1.6×10^8** (≈**0.2 dB/m**) [[6]](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2022APLP....7h1301S/abstract), [[7]](https://pubs.aip.org/aip/app/article/7/8/081301/2835188/Reduced-material-loss-in-thin-film-lithium-niobate). Even if that work targets ion‑slicing material loss rather than etch damage, it strongly supports annealing as a route to reduce defect‑related absorption once scattering is under control.

**How to use this in practice (typical flow logic):**
1) Strip residues/redep first (so you don’t “bake in” contamination).
2) Anneal in an oxidizing ambient (O2/air) if your concern includes oxygen‑vacancy‑type defects / absorption.
3) Only then deposit your final cladding/passivation (to avoid hydrogen/charge trap creation during deposition changing your anneal benefit).

### B. Nitrogen annealing can strongly affect photorefraction and interface states
A detailed (preprint) study on PR in thin‑film LN ring resonators shows that dielectric cladding and process steps can “activate” PR, and that **500 °C N2 anneals (1 h)** can suppress PR again after oxide deposition/removal cycles [[38]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2012.12671). They report that removing SiO2 cladding plus appropriate heat treatment suppressed relative PR‑induced resonance shifts by **>2 orders of magnitude** at the same intracavity power [[38]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2012.12671). While not strictly “etch damage,” it is a *post‑etch* recovery lever because the PR was strongly modulated by post‑etch deposition/removal and thermal steps.

### C. Hydrogen/forming‑gas anneals and SC‑1 cleans appear in LN processing (but validate optical impact)
A review excerpt describes a post‑process sequence including **10% H2 + Ar at 400 °C** followed by **SC‑1 at 85 °C** (10 min) as part of LN nanodevice processing [[39]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10609314/). Because this is presented in an overview context and not tied (in the captured excerpt) to optical loss/Q outcomes, treat it as a *possible knob*—useful for certain defect/interface chemistries—but one that must be validated carefully in photonic devices (hydrogen can also change charge dynamics and PR behavior depending on interfaces).

---

## 5) Post‑etch “damage removal” and smoothing by controlled plasma techniques (ALE / shallow cleanup etches)

If you suspect an ion‑damaged skin or redeposition layer that is too thin/tenacious for wet stripping—or you want smoothing without wet processing—**atomic layer etching (ALE)** and gentle cleanup etches are emerging options.

### A. Shallow “cleanup etch” to improve symmetry/roughness (x‑cut anisotropy case)
For x‑cut LNOI rib waveguides, one approach combined:
* primary Ar ICP‑RIE,
* wet clean (NH4OH/H2O2),
* then a **shallow Ar/O2 etch** to improve sidewall symmetry and smoothness (addressing anisotropic sidewall differences between +z and –z) [[15]](https://ksp.etri.re.kr/ksp/article/file/70162.pdf).

This is a pragmatic “post‑etch repair etch”: a light second etch to remove residues and even out roughness, with the risk that extra plasma exposure can add damage if not kept shallow/low‑energy.

### B. ALE for LN: cyclic chemistries that reduce redeposition and can smooth milled sidewalls
Two ALE directions appear in the retrieved literature:

1) **Directional ALE using HBr/BCl3/Ar + Ar steps** (preprint):  
A cyclic process was reported where a halogen‑plasma “modify” step is followed by a controlled “remove” step, with the claim that Br chemistry decreases redeposition vs F/Cl due to higher vapor pressure products [[32]](https://www.alphaxiv.org/abs/2511.01825). They report **no ARDE down to 150 nm gaps** for a 220 nm etch depth, unlike ion milling [[32]](https://www.alphaxiv.org/abs/2511.01825). This is directly relevant if your nonlinear photonics design needs dense gratings/metasurfaces where ARDE and redeposition are killers.

2) **Isotropic ALE on MgO:LN using sequential H2 and SF6/Ar plasmas**, with an alternative **Cl2/BCl3** plasma option (peer‑reviewed JVST A):  
This ALE work reports that ALE can reduce sidewall roughness of TFLN waveguides etched by physical Ar+ milling by **~30%** without additional wet processing [[36]](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2023arXiv231010592C/abstract). It also explicitly notes an ALE variant using sequential H2 plasma exposure followed by **Cl2/BCl3** exposure (recipe details included in the paper excerpt) [[35]](https://pubs.aip.org/avs/jva/article-pdf/doi/10.1116/6.0003962/20205711/062603_1_6.0003962.pdf).

**Why this matters for “damage after etch”:** ALE can act like a *digital, low‑damage polishing step*—removing a thin damaged/redep layer with higher controllability than continuous RIE and potentially lower added roughness than aggressive wet etches.

---

## 6) Passivation / cladding choices can either hide or amplify “etch damage” (especially via PR and charging)

Even if you perfectly smooth sidewalls, thin‑film LN performance can still be limited by **interfaces** (charge traps, diffusion, PR activation). So “mitigation after plasma etch” also includes what you put on the LN afterward and how you treat it thermally.

### A. Dielectric cladding can strongly influence photorefraction
A peer‑reviewed abstract reports that dielectric cladding has significant influence on PR in LN microrings and that removing dielectric cladding can effectively mitigate PR [[10]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33726085/). The preprint study discussed above provides a detailed process narrative consistent with this: PECVD SiO2 cladding activated strong PR distortions; removing cladding and annealing reduced PR dramatically [[38]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2012.12671).

**Actionable implication:** If your nonlinear process is PR‑limited (e.g., SHG/comb thresholds), consider:
* air‑clad or weakly cladded designs,
* alternative claddings with lower trap densities,
* and/or anneal sequences that stabilize interfaces after deposition/etching.

### B. SiO2 cladding is often used for protection and mode shaping, but may require anneal tuning
One wafer‑scale flow uses PECVD SiO2 cladding as protection and to adjust mode distribution [[14]](https://www.db-thueringen.de/servlets/MCRFileNodeServlet/dbt_derivate_00070614/ome-15-2-299.pdf), [[21]](https://arxiv.org/html/2407.09208v1). But oxide deposition/removal steps can also create interface states that change PR/charging (as evidenced by PR activation cycles) [[38]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2012.12671). The practical lesson is not “never clad,” but “clad + anneal + characterize PR and drift.”

---

## 7) Benchmarks: what “good mitigation” looks like numerically (so you know if your fix worked)

You can use these as sanity checks when you change post‑etch recovery:

* **Ultra‑high‑Q etched TFLN resonators** with dry etch + cleaning + anneal: intrinsic Q up to **29.3 million**, corresponding to **1.3 dB/m** propagation loss (Ar ICP‑RIE based, then chemical cleaning and anneal) [[16]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2402.16161).  
* **Wafer‑scale CHF3 ICP‑RIE** waveguides with strong post‑etch cleaning + O2 anneal: straight waveguide loss around **2 dB/m** [[14]](https://www.db-thueringen.de/servlets/MCRFileNodeServlet/dbt_derivate_00070614/ome-15-2-299.pdf), [[21]](https://arxiv.org/html/2407.09208v1).  
* **Ar ICP‑RIE rib waveguides** with wet clean + shallow Ar/O2 symmetry‑improvement etch: **0.16 dB/cm** and microring intrinsic Q ~**2.58×10^6** [[15]](https://ksp.etri.re.kr/ksp/article/file/70162.pdf).  
* **Material‑limited thin‑film LN** after anneal: Q ~**1.6×10^8** (~**0.2 dB/m**) [[6]](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2022APLP....7h1301S/abstract), [[7]](https://pubs.aip.org/aip/app/article/7/8/081301/2835188/Reduced-material-loss-in-thin-film-lithium-niobate) (useful as a “floor” target once scattering is very low).

If your post‑etch “mitigation” improves SEM aesthetics but doesn’t move ring Q/loss toward these regimes (for comparable geometry), it may not be addressing the dominant limiter.

---

## 8) Recommended validation workflow (to ensure you’re fixing *material damage* and not just changing coupling)

A robust “damage mitigation” claim in LN should separate: **sidewall scattering**, **material absorption/defect loss**, and **coupling loss**. Practical metrology stack:

1) **SEM/FIB cross‑sections**: verify redeposition presence/absence and trenching; redeposition can be composition/phase‑distinct [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11501321/), [[13]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11194688/).  
2) **AFM** on top surface and (if possible) sidewall roughness proxies; connect to Q‑scaling with width (narrower guides are more sidewall‑limited) (roughness/Q linkage is discussed in preprint form) [[9]](https://arxiv.org/html/2505.01913v3).  
3) **Ring resonator Q statistics** across widths/radii: wide racetracks reduce sidewall overlap; if Q jumps with width, you’re sidewall‑limited [[16]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2402.16161).  
4) **XPS/Raman/IR** if you suspect chemical modification: oxygen plasma activation demonstrably changes LN surface composition detectable by XPS/Raman/IR [[31]](http://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2025PSSAR.22200924S/abstract).  
5) **PR characterization** under realistic intracavity power; cladding/interface steps can dominate PR (and anneals can reset it) [[10]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33726085/), [[38]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2012.12671).  
6) **Before/after anneal comparison** on the *same wafer* to isolate anneal‑healing contributions (material loss floor) [[6]](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2022APLP....7h1301S/abstract), [[7]](https://pubs.aip.org/aip/app/article/7/8/081301/2835188/Reduced-material-loss-in-thin-film-lithium-niobate).

---

## 9) Putting it together: practical “post‑etch mitigation recipes” (modular)

Below are *modular* mitigation packages you can combine depending on whether you’re Ar‑etched or F‑etched, and whether PR/stability matters more than absolute loss.

### Package A (Ar ICP‑RIE, sidewall‑scattering dominated)
1) Process‑level redep suppression: bias/pressure optimization toward redeposition‑free regime [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11501321/) + endpoint control to avoid over‑etch [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11501321/).  
2) Post‑etch redep strip: NH4OH/H2O2 clean (example recipe above) [[15]](https://ksp.etri.re.kr/ksp/article/file/70162.pdf).  
3) Optional shallow cleanup etch (Ar/O2) for symmetry/roughness (x‑cut anisotropy case) [[15]](https://ksp.etri.re.kr/ksp/article/file/70162.pdf).  
4) Anneal (O2 if chasing absorption floor; N2 if chasing PR/interface reset—validate for your stack) [[14]](https://www.db-thueringen.de/servlets/MCRFileNodeServlet/dbt_derivate_00070614/ome-15-2-299.pdf), [[21]](https://arxiv.org/html/2407.09208v1), [[38]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2012.12671).  

### Package B (CHF3 / fluorine ICP‑RIE, residue/passivation risk)
1) Temperature‑controlled etch to suppress residual adsorption (reported effective in CHF3 flow) [[14]](https://www.db-thueringen.de/servlets/MCRFileNodeServlet/dbt_derivate_00070614/ome-15-2-299.pdf), [[21]](https://arxiv.org/html/2407.09208v1).  
2) Strong residue/passivation removal: megasonic acid clean approach (as reported) [[14]](https://www.db-thueringen.de/servlets/MCRFileNodeServlet/dbt_derivate_00070614/ome-15-2-299.pdf), [[21]](https://arxiv.org/html/2407.09208v1).  
3) O2 anneal 520 °C/2 h to improve crystallinity and reduce loss [[14]](https://www.db-thueringen.de/servlets/MCRFileNodeServlet/dbt_derivate_00070614/ome-15-2-299.pdf), [[21]](https://arxiv.org/html/2407.09208v1).  
4) Cladding choice + PR validation (dielectric cladding can worsen PR; removal can mitigate) [[10]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33726085/), [[38]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2012.12671).

### Package C (Post‑etch plasma “polishing” instead of wet chemistry)
1) Use ALE as a controlled smoothing/damage‑removal step after Ar milling: reported ~30% roughness reduction without wet processing [[36]](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2023arXiv231010592C/abstract).  
2) If needed for dense nanostructures: directional ALE options claiming reduced redeposition and improved small‑gap behavior (preprint) [[32]](https://www.alphaxiv.org/abs/2511.01825).  

---

## 10) Open problems / pitfalls (what still commonly breaks even with “good” mitigation)
* **Shared-tool contamination sensitivity:** redeposition/micromasking can be driven by sputtered contaminants from chamber hardware; several works emphasize cleaning/chemistry control to maintain reproducibility [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11501321/), [[13]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11194688/).  
* **Tradeoff between “remove redep” and “create damage”:** high DC bias can reduce redeposition [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11501321/) but raises risk of trenching/energetic damage (noted explicitly) [[15]](https://ksp.etri.re.kr/ksp/article/file/70162.pdf).  
* **Cladding/interface states dominate PR and drift:** you can “fix” roughness yet still be PR‑limited or DC‑drift‑limited if interfaces are activated by deposition/removal steps [[10]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33726085/), [[38]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2012.12671), [[3]](https://assets-eu.researchsquare.com/files/rs-5775859/v1_covered_bb4f8f8c-5cc6-4189-b443-456f929df438.pdf).  
* **Evidence gaps:** cryogenic LN etch and pulsed-plasma mitigation are not well supported in the retrieved material set (i.e., you should rely on direct experimental validation if you pursue them, rather than expecting established best‑practice recipes from this specific literature subset).

---

### Bottom line (decision tree)
*If your loss/Q is dominated by scattering:* prioritize redeposition‑free etching conditions + gentle residue removal (avoid roughening cleans) + optional ALE smoothing [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11501321/), [[15]](https://ksp.etri.re.kr/ksp/article/file/70162.pdf), [[36]](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2023arXiv231010592C/abstract).  
*If your device is PR‑limited or unstable under high optical power/DC bias:* focus on interface/cladding/anneal sequences (sometimes cladding removal + anneal is the strongest lever) [[10]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33726085/), [[38]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2012.12671).  
*If you’re already near scattering limits:* annealing to reduce material/defect loss becomes essential (material‑limited Q can be dramatically anneal‑improved) [[6]](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2022APLP....7h1301S/abstract), [[7]](https://pubs.aip.org/aip/app/article/7/8/081301/2835188/Reduced-material-loss-in-thin-film-lithium-niobate), [[14]](https://www.db-thueringen.de/servlets/MCRFileNodeServlet/dbt_derivate_00070614/ome-15-2-299.pdf).
